J'accompagne les familles, les premiers: I guide families, first-time buyers and investors in Sainte-Thérèse, Blainville, Rosemère and Lorraine through what is often the biggest financial decision of their lives. In concrete terms, that means three things: I know each neighbourhood well, I set a price that holds up thanks to recent comparable sales, and I stay transparent with you from the first call to the signing at the notary's.

L'inspection préachat, c'est votre derni: The pre-purchase inspection is your last protection before signing a commitment of several hundred thousand dollars. Yet many buyers choose to waive it to “win the bidding war” — a risky bet that must be weighed clear-headedly.
L'inventaire reste structurellement limi: Inventory remains structurally limited — scarcity supports prices on single-family homes and plexes, despite the recent supply of new condos.
L'unifamiliale 1980-2000 sur terrain pay: The 1980-2000 single-family home on a landscaped lot dominates. Renovated bungalows attract retirees. The market for recent builds is limited by land scarcity. See
L'unifamiliale en cottage (typologie 199: The cottage-style single-family home (1990-2010) remains Blainville's engine, especially in Fontainebleau and Chambéry. The new condo is gaining ground near the REM and commercial districts. The rental-investment plex is growing fast (+10% in 12 months). See
L'unifamiliale sur grand terrain (15 000: The single-family home on a large lot (15,000+ sq ft) remains the heart of the Rosemère market, especially in Bois-Franc and Domaine-du-Parc. Waterfront properties on Grande-Côte form a distinct segment ($1.2-2M). The high-end condo near Highway 640 is growing. See

La fenêtre optimale s'ouvre fin avril. P: The optimal window opens in late April. Peak in mid-May in Sainte-Thérèse, early June in Blainville. If you plan to sell in 2026, now is when it's decided — summer always slows down.

Lorraine attire les acheteurs qui cherch: Lorraine attracts buyers looking for a living environment distinct from the classic suburban grid: winding streets, mature trees, consistent architecture. The market there is quieter but more stable than in neighbouring cities — less seller pressure, more buyer loyalty.
